# amieval — working tree

This whole tree is the Composable Beliefs ecosystem and its satellites. The durable
state of all work here lives in **belief graphs**, never in prose, memory, or the
prompt that launched you (cb:a509). Trust the graph over anything this file, a handoff,
or a task description says - other sessions write these repos concurrently.

## Session start - do this before acting on any task

A session resumes from the graph, never from the prompt. Before touching anything,
regardless of which subdirectory you started in:

1. `cd composable-beliefs` and follow its `CLAUDE.md` **"Session start"** - that is the
   SSOT for the ritual. Pull the repos you will touch first.
2. Query the desk for live obligations: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete`,
   and `mix bs stale --cascade` for review flags.
3. If you were handed a task id, bootstrap from it: `mix bs show <id>`, then follow its
   deps and `document:`/`plan:` artifacts to the records that ground it.

Working from the prompt alone instead of querying the live graph is the known failure
mode (agent-behavior:a165). This file only routes you to the graph; the graph decides.

## The repos under this tree

- `composable-beliefs/` - the framework, its `cb:` design graph, the in-repo `okfx:`
  (the OKF/Knowledge extension under `okf/`) and `codepath:` collections, and all the
  `mix bs` / write-flow tooling.
- `belief-collections/` - worked-example and method collections; `collections.json` is
  the namespace -> path resolution map.
- `amieval-direction/` and `cb-direction/` - PRIVATE direction repos. Strategy,
  positioning, competitor/market analysis route here as dated `direction/` docs, never
  to a public surface (README, docs, positions, a public graph, a blog draft). CB-the-
  system's direction -> `cb-direction`; the broader operation -> `amieval-direction`.
- Other siblings (`bench/`, `evals/`, `plan-app/`, `cb-site/`, `eval-tut/`, …) are
  satellites; each carries its own `CLAUDE.md` where it has one.

## Notes

- This file is hand-written for now. When the cross-project directive graph lands
  (cb:a543) it should compile from the graph the way the framework `CLAUDE.md` does
  (cb:c062), so this prose stops being a hand-edit/drift risk (cb:a386).
- Never use emdashes; use hyphens.

content.text:
Resolved — and the answer turned out to be cleaner than "wait for a generator."

**What happened:** the deferral note was just wrong. The real governing principle already exists — **`cb:a466`** ("generated prose compiles where there's an oracle to diff; hand-written prose is reserved for a distinct audience and must never freeze drifting lists, it points at the live source"). Under `a466`, `amieval/CLAUDE.md` is **correctly hand-written by design** — it routes to the graph instead of embedding directives. There was never a `cb:a543` dependency.

So I:

attachment.snippet:
1	<!-- Generated by `mix cb.generate.claude_md --beliefs okf/beliefs.json` from the okfx: DAG. Do not edit; run the task to regenerate. -->
2	
3	# CLAUDE.md - okf/ (the OKF integration extension)
4	
5	This directory is the Knowledge methodology: a portable OKF-based format for knowledge across all my repos and productivity domains, with Composable Beliefs as an opt-in rigor ceiling. It is CB's OKF integration extension, folded into composable-beliefs beside the tooling that implements it (it used to be the standalone `knowledge` repo).
6	
7	## Orientation
8	
9	If you are a fresh agent pointed here, route by intent: to **adopt this methodology** read [`ADOPT.md`](ADOPT.md); to **convert an existing store** read [`CONVERT.md`](CONVERT.md); to **understand the format** read [`standard/KNOWLEDGE.md`](standard/KNOWLEDGE.md) first, then `standard/types.md`, `standard/frontmatter.md`, and `standard/tiers.md`; to **understand why it is shaped this way** read [`meta/`](meta/), the foundational design record (the originating session, the OKF-vs-CB analysis, the two-tier decision); to **see it used** open [`demo/`](demo/), a synthetic example bundle, starting at [`demo/manifest.json`](demo/manifest.json).
10	
11	## Tooling
12	
13	The tooling lives alongside this directory in composable-beliefs (`mix knowledge.*`, `lib/cb/knowledge/*`) as CB's OKF integration layer; run the mix tasks from the repo root against a bundle path under `okf/`. After any change to an OKF bundle, regenerate its manifest, e.g. `mix knowledge.manifest okf/demo` (and `mix knowledge.manifest okf/meta`), then validate with `mix knowledge.validate okf/<bundle>`.
14	
15	## The okfx graph is not an OKF bundle
16	
17	knowledge/beliefs/ is the methodology's own CB belief graph (beliefs.json + a CB graph manifest), NOT an OKF bundle. Never run the OKF tooling (mix knowledge.manifest / mix knowledge.validate) against it: the validator reports it stale because it has zero .md docs, and the manifest generator would overwrite its CB graph descriptor with an empty OKF manifest. It is read and written only through the CB graph tooling (mix bs, the cb.preflight/import write flow). Per standard/KNOWLEDGE.md section 1.1 (the standard owns format rules; the consumer owns operational directives), this guard is consumer-owned and lives here in the graph, not duplicated as prose in CLAUDE.md.
18	
19	## Standard, not an app
20	
21	This is a standard, not an app: [`conformance/`](conformance/) defines the format as behaviour (fixtures plus normative `expected/*.json`). The single implementation (`mix knowledge.manifest` / `mix knowledge.validate`, plus the `knowledge.emit` / `knowledge.ingest` bridge) is held to that corpus by the repo's ExUnit conformance test (`test/cb/okf_conformance_test.exs`), which must stay green.
22	
23	## Core rule
24	
25	Core rule: everything defaults to the OKF floor (plain markdown plus frontmatter plus a generated manifest). Promote a document to the CB ceiling (`tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`) only when it trips the boundary test in `standard/tiers.md`.

diff --git a/lib/mix/tasks/cb.generate.claude_md.ex b/lib/mix/tasks/cb.generate.claude_md.ex
index 3540899..837f5af 100644
--- a/lib/mix/tasks/cb.generate.claude_md.ex
+++ b/lib/mix/tasks/cb.generate.claude_md.ex
@@ -12,6 +12,15 @@ defmodule Mix.Tasks.Cb.Generate.ClaudeMd do
       mix cb.generate.claude_md          - generate the file
       mix cb.generate.claude_md --check  - diff against current; no write
 
+  `--beliefs PATH` points the generator at an alternate belief graph for
+  one invocation (the same override the belief shell and the cb.preflight/
+  import write flow take), so a collection that carries its own
+  `output:claude-md` contract compiles its own file. For example,
+  `mix cb.generate.claude_md --beliefs okf/beliefs.json` compiles
+  `okf/CLAUDE.md` from the okfx: graph while the no-arg invocation keeps
+  compiling the framework CLAUDE.md from cb:. The single-active-target rule
+  is enforced per store, so each graph must carry exactly one.
+
   ## Exit codes
 
   0 = generated or check passed, 1 = errors or check failed
@@ -33,9 +42,11 @@ defmodule Mix.Tasks.Cb.Generate.ClaudeMd do
 
   @impl Mix.Task
   def run(args) do
-    {opts, _, _} = OptionParser.parse(args, strict: [check: :boolean])
+    {opts, _, _} = OptionParser.parse(args, strict: [check: :boolean, beliefs: :string])
     check? = opts[:check] || false
 
+    if path = opts[:beliefs], do: Application.put_env(:cb, :beliefs_path, path)
+
     root = CB.repo_root()
 
     with {:ok, [target | _] = targets, all} <- OutputTarget.find_targets(tag: @tag),

  @moduledoc """
  #{@shortdoc}

      mix knowledge.manifest <bundle-root>          # write <root>/manifest.json
      mix knowledge.manifest <bundle-root> --check  # exit 1 if the manifest is stale

  Part of CB's OKF integration layer. Byte-compatible with the Python reference
  tool in the `knowledge` standard repo.
  """
  use Mix.Task

  @impl Mix.Task
  def run(argv) do
    {opts, rest, _} = OptionParser.parse(argv, switches: [check: :boolean])
    root = List.first(rest) || Mix.raise("usage: mix knowledge.manifest <bundle-root> [--check]")
    content = CB.Knowledge.Manifest.render(root)
    out = Path.join(root, "manifest.json")

content.thinking:
The problem is that `$FILES` is being treated as a single argument instead of being word-split by the shell. Both perl and ugrep are receiving the entire list as one filename, which is why they're complaining they can't open it. This could be happening because of how the variable is being expanded—either the newlines in the multi-line string aren't triggering word splitting, or there's something about the quoting or IFS that's preventing it.

Actually, wait—the user's shell is zsh, and zsh doesn't do word splitting on unquoted parameter expansion by default like bash does. That's the issue. I can work around this by using globs directly instead of a variable, since glob patterns expand fine in zsh. So I'll inline the file patterns directly into the perl command: the lib/cb/okf/*.ex files, lib/mix/tasks/okf.*.ex, the test files. That way I avoid the variable word-split problem entirely and can apply the rename.

file.content:
defmodule CB.KnowledgeTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true

  alias CB.Okf.{Frontmatter, Manifest, Validate}

  setup do
    root = Path.join(System.tmp_dir!(), "kn_#{System.unique_integer([:positive])}")
    File.mkdir_p!(root)
    on_exit(fn -> File.rm_rf(root) end)
    {:ok, root: root}
  end

  defp write(root, rel, contents) do
    path = Path.join(root, rel)
    File.mkdir_p!(Path.dirname(path))

toolUseResult.originalFile:
defmodule CB.KnowledgeTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true

  alias CB.Okf.{Frontmatter, Manifest, Validate}

  setup do
    root = Path.join(System.tmp_dir!(), "kn_#{System.unique_integer([:positive])}")
    File.mkdir_p!(root)
    on_exit(fn -> File.rm_rf(root) end)
    {:ok, root: root}
  end

  defp write(root, rel, contents) do
    path = Path.join(root, rel)
    File.mkdir_p!(Path.dirname(path))
    File.write!(path, contents)
  end

  defp doc(fields, body \\ "Body.\n") do
    fm = Enum.map_join(fields, "\n", fn {k, v} -> "#{k}: #{v}" end)
    "---\n#{fm}\n---\n\n#{body}"
  end

  test "frontmatter parses the documented subset" do
    fm =
      Frontmatter.parse("""
      ---
      type: concept
      title: T
      description: >
        folded line one
        line two
      tags: [a, b]
      deps:
        - x:1
        - x:2
      ---
      body
      """)

    assert fm["type"] == "concept"
    assert fm["description"] == "folded line one line two"
    assert fm["tags"] == ["a", "b"]
    assert fm["deps"] == ["x:1", "x:2"]
  end

  test "no frontmatter yields empty map" do
    assert Frontmatter.parse("# just markdown\n") == %{}
  end

  test "valid bundle passes with fresh manifest", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "index", title: "Idx",
      description: "Use when orienting to this valid test bundle root index."))

    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert errors == []
    assert warnings == []
  end

  test "flags bad type, short description, stale manifest", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), "{}\n")

    {errors, _warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    codes = MapSet.new(errors, & &1.code)
    assert "type_not_in_taxonomy" in codes
    assert "description_missing_or_short" in codes
    assert "manifest_stale" in codes
  end

  test "cb-tier doc without id is flagged; resolving deps pass", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "index", title: "Idx",
      description: "Use when orienting to the CB-tier test bundle index page."))
    write(root, "base.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Base",
      description: "Use when you need the base belief the derived one depends upon.",
      tier: "cb", id: "t:base"))
    write(root, "derived.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Derived",
      description: "Use when you need the derived belief composed from the base one.",
      tier: "cb", id: "t:derived", deps: "[t:base]"))

    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert errors == []
    assert warnings == []

    # now break the id on base
    write(root, "base.md", doc(type: "concept", title: "Base",
      description: "Use when you need the base belief the derived one depends upon.",
      tier: "cb"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    assert Enum.any?(errors, &(&1.code == "cb_tier_missing_id"))
    assert Enum.any?(warnings, &(&1.code == "dep_unresolved_in_bundle"))
  end

  test "to_contract is deterministic and sorted", %{root: root} do
    write(root, "index.md", doc(type: "gizmo", title: "Bad", description: "short"))
    File.write!(Path.join(root, "manifest.json"), Manifest.render(root))
    {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(root)
    json = Validate.to_contract(errors, warnings)
    decoded = Jason.decode!(json)

    assert decoded["ok"] == false
    codes = Enum.map(decoded["errors"], & &1["code"])
    assert codes == Enum.sort(codes)
  end

  describe "adapter" do
    defp sample_beliefs do
      [
        %{
          "id" => "cb:a001",
          "type" => "primitive",
          "kind" => "convention",
          "claim" => "The standard loan period for circulating items is twenty-one days.",
          "artifact" => "document:policy.md",
          "deps" => [],
          "status" => "active",
          "created" => "2026-01-05",
          "tags" => ["policy"]
        },
        %{
          "id" => "cb:a002",
          "type" => "inference",
          "claim" => "Because the loan period is twenty-one days, holds placed today expire mid-month.",
          "deps" => ["cb:a001"],
          "status" => "active",
          "created" => "2026-01-06",
          "tags" => []
        }
      ]
    end

    test "emit produces a bundle that validates green", %{root: root} do
      out = Path.join(root, "okf")
      assert {:ok, 2} = CB.Okf.Emit.bundle(sample_beliefs(), out)
      assert File.exists?(Path.join(out, "cb-a001.md"))
      assert File.read!(Path.join(out, "cb-a002.md")) =~ "[cb:a001](cb-a001.md)"

      {errors, warnings} = Validate.run(out)
      assert errors == []
      assert warnings == []
    end

    test "ingest lands every doc as an attributable primitive", %{root: root} do
      out = Path.join(root, "okf")
      CB.Okf.Emit.bundle(sample_beliefs(), out)

      ingested = CB.Okf.Ingest.beliefs(out, "lib")
      assert length(ingested) == 2
      assert Enum.all?(ingested, &(&1["type"] == "primitive"))
      assert Enum.all?(ingested, &String.starts_with?(&1["artifact"], "document:"))
      assert Enum.all?(ingested, &(&1["deps"] == []))
    end
  end
end

attachment.snippet:
1	---
2	type: spec
3	title: The Knowledge Methodology
4	description: The canonical spec — three layers, the frontmatter spine, the manifest index, and the OKF/CB two-tier rule. Read this first when adopting or converting.
5	tags: [spec, okf, cb, methodology]
6	status: active
7	timestamp: 2026-06-21
8	id: spec:knowledge
9	---
10	
11	# The Knowledge Methodology
12	
13	## 1. Lineage (what this is built from)
14	
15	| Layer | Source | What we take |
16	|---|---|---|
17	| Format | **OKF** (Open Knowledge Format) | markdown + YAML frontmatter, directory hierarchy, `index.md` progressive disclosure, cross-links, "format not platform" |
18	| Synthesis discipline | **Karpathy LLM-wiki** | three layers (raw → wiki → schema), ingest-time synthesis, `index.md`/`log.md`, compounding artifact |
19	| Epistemic rigor (opt-in) | **Composable Beliefs (CB)** | typed claims, stable ids, typed `deps`, dated supersession, staleness propagation, contracts |
20	
21	The floor is OKF-compliant: every file is plain markdown any editor renders and any
22	search tool indexes. The ceiling (CB) is **additive frontmatter** on the same files —
23	it never breaks OKF compatibility (see §6).
24	
25	### Methodology vs standard
26	
27	This repo is the **methodology** (the umbrella: format, practices, the two-tier
28	philosophy, thread persistence, the ADOPT/CONVERT workflow). The conformance-tested
29	**format standard** is the subset defined in `standard/` and checked by `conformance/`.
30	Methodology ⊇ standard: not everything here is machine-enforced (the two-tier rule and
31	thread persistence are practice, not conformance checks).
32	
33	### What the standard owns vs what consumers own
34	
35	A governing boundary, so rules don't get duplicated or misplaced:
36	
37	- **Format rules** (how a doc is shaped: types, frontmatter, manifest, links, ids, and
38	  how threads persist) are **owned here**, in the standard. One source of truth.
39	- **Operational directives** (when/how an agent *does* something — e.g. "at session end,
40	  duplicate the transcript and write the source+thread pair") are **owned by the
41	  consumer**. In the CB world that means a directive *belief in the graph* (CB renders
42	  directives live from the DAG rather than caching them to a file), which `deps`/`artifact`
43	  grounds back to the relevant doc here. Consumers reference the standard; they do not
44	  re-state its rules.
45	
46	## 2. The three layers
47	
48	This is the Karpathy shape, made concrete.
49	
50	```
51	┌─ Layer 3: SCHEMA ──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
52	│  This spec + CLAUDE.md conventions. How the bundle is built.    │
53	├─ Layer 2: SYNTHESIS (the "wiki") ──────────────────────────────┤
54	│  Compiled concept docs. Maintained at ingest time. OKF markdown.│
55	│  ← Both agent AND human read THIS first. ─────────────────────  │
56	├─ Layer 1: SOURCES ─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
57	│  Immutable captures (type: source). Drilled into only when the  │
58	│  synthesis is insufficient or provenance is in question.        │
59	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
60	```
61	
62	**There is no separate "human wiki."** The synthesis docs *are* the wiki, and they are
63	OKF markdown that both the agent and a human read. A rendered website (e.g. the OKF
64	HTML visualizer) is just `render(bundle)` — a throwaway view, never authored, never a
65	source of truth.
66	
67	**Default read path:** agent loads `manifest.json` → matches by `description` → loads
68	the synthesis docs it needs → drills to Layer-1 sources only for provenance or detail.
69	
70	## 3. The frontmatter spine
71	
72	Every document carries the same frontmatter spine. Only `type` is strictly required
73	(OKF rule); the rest are strongly recommended. Full field reference:
74	[`frontmatter.md`](frontmatter.md).
75	
76	```yaml
77	---
78	type: concept            # REQUIRED. one of the taxonomy types (see types.md)
79	title: Household cashflow # human label
80	description: >           # RELEVANCE HOOK — see §4. the most important field.
81	  Use when reasoning about monthly money in/out, recurring obligations, or runway.
82	tags: [finances, recurring, runway]
83	status: active           # active | superseded | retracted | draft
84	timestamp: 2026-06-21    # last meaningful update (ISO date)
85	# --- tier-2 (CB) fields, optional, additive ---
86	id: fin:cashflow         # stable identity (see §6)
87	deps: [fin:income-sources, fin:fixed-costs]   # typed dependencies
88	---
89	```
90	
91	## 4. `description` is a relevance hook, not a summary
92	
93	This is the mechanism that lets an agent understand what a doc holds **without loading
94	it** — the same trick as a Claude skill's `description` or a `MEMORY.md` one-liner.
95	
96	- Write it as **"Use when…" / "Covers…"** — what question this doc answers.
97	- ≤ 2 lines. If you need more, the doc is doing too much; split it.
98	- It must be true and discriminating: an agent decides *load or skip* from this line
99	  alone, so a vague description silently costs context on every traversal.
100	
101	Bad: `description: Notes about money.`
102	Good: `description: Use when reasoning about monthly money in/out, recurring obligations, or runway.`
103	
104	## 5. The manifest (the summary index)
105	
106	`bundle/manifest.json` is a **generated** harvest of every doc's frontmatter into one
107	small file the agent loads first. It is the skills-style summary layer. Contract:
108	
109	> **This is this standard's addition, not OKF-native.** OKF's entry mechanism is
110	> `index.md` (human progressive disclosure); the generated `manifest.json` agent-index is
111	> a bespoke extension this methodology layers on top — which is why the lineage table (§1)
112	> credits OKF with `index.md` but not the manifest. It is the concrete reason the
113	> collection's namespace is `okfx:` (extends OKF) rather than `okf:`.
114	
115	- Produced by `mix okf.manifest <root>` (in the `composable-beliefs` repo); **never hand-edited**.
116	- One entry per `.md` doc: `path`, `type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`, `status`,
117	  `timestamp`, and (if present) `id`, `deps`, `tier`.
118	- Small by construction — bodies are excluded. An agent reads the whole manifest
119	  cheaply, then loads only the bodies whose `description` matches its task.
120	- Regenerated on every write to the bundle (CI or a pre-commit hook; for now, run the
121	  tool by hand — see ADOPT.md).
122	
123	`index.md` files complement the manifest for **human** progressive disclosure (browse
124	a folder, read its index, descend). The manifest is for the agent; `index.md` is for
125	the human walking the tree. Both are derived from the same docs.
126	
127	## 6. Identity: path-canonical, id-additive
128	
129	OKF identity is the **file path**, and cross-links are plain markdown links to paths —
130	so a generic OKF consumer navigates with zero integration. Keep that as canonical.
131	
132	`id` is an **additive** tier-2 field (legal OKF — only `type` is required). Your tooling
133	uses it for two things generic tools can't do:
134	
135	1. **Typed `deps`** that survive prose (the CB edge), and
136	2. **Rebinding path links after a rename** — the id is the stable anchor.
137	
138	Rule: **never drop the path links.** A doc that links *only* by id is no longer OKF.
139	So: path links canonical (OKF stays intact), id additive (CB tier rides on top). `id`
140	is the first CB feature worth pulling down into the floor, because these repos

142

144	
145	**Default (OKF/Karpathy floor):** when a new source lands, the agent updates the
146	relevant concept docs in place — integrating the new fact, resolving contradictions by
147	rewriting, refreshing cross-links and the folder `index.md`. Prose, model-maintained.
148	This is enough for almost everything.

1	# CONVERT — migrating an existing knowledge store to this format
2	
3	You are an agent told: *"convert this existing knowledge store to this format."* The
4	store may be ad-hoc markdown, a `plan.md`/`DESIGN.md` sprawl, a notes folder, a JSON
5	belief file, or some mix. Convert **without losing provenance** and **without inventing
6	rigor that wasn't there.**
7	
8	**Each mutating step ends in a gate you run — do not proceed past a failing gate.**
9	Self-verification commands (no tooling is copied into the target repo — run the
10	`mix okf.*` tasks from the `composable-beliefs` repo root against the bundle path):
11	
12	```
13	mix okf.manifest <root>   # (re)generate <root>/manifest.json
14	mix okf.validate <root>   # exit 0 = pass; FAIL: lines stop you
15	```
16	
17	## 0. Read the spec
18	[`standard/KNOWLEDGE.md`](standard/KNOWLEDGE.md), then [`standard/types.md`](standard/types.md) and
19	[`standard/tiers.md`](standard/tiers.md).
20	
21	**Gate (self-check):** you must be able to name which artifact kinds map to which `type`,
22	and the rule for what stays on the OKF floor. If not, re-read `types.md`/`tiers.md`.
23	
24	## 1. Snapshot, then inventory (transform comes later)
25	First ensure the original is recoverable: confirm the store is committed in git (or copy
26	it aside). Then walk it and record, for each artifact: current path, what it actually is,
27	and target `type`. Write the inventory as a `type: analysis` doc in `<root>/meta/` so the
28	conversion itself is provenanced.
29	
30	Common mappings:
31	| You find | Maps to |
32	|---|---|
33	| `README.md`, runbooks, how-tos | `reference` |
34	| `plan.md`, `USER_STORIES.md`, roadmap | `plan` |
35	| dated `analyses/*.md`, investigations | `analysis` |
36	| ingested articles, transcripts, statements | `source` (immutable) |
37	| pages about an entity/topic, synthesized notes | `concept` |
38	| settled decisions, ADRs | `position` |
39	| session logs, chat exports | `thread` |
40	| a JSON belief/assertion file | keep as CB store; **emit** `concept`/`source` docs from it (see §4) |
41	
42	**Gate:** `git status` shows the original tracked/clean (recoverable), and the inventory
43	doc exists. Do not edit any source file until both are true.
44	
45	## 2. Add the frontmatter spine, don't rewrite bodies
46	For each file: prepend the spine (`type`, `title`, `description`, `tags`, `status`,
47	`timestamp`). **Write a real relevance hook for `description`** (≥20 chars, "Use when…")
48	— the highest-value part of the conversion. Preserve original dates in `timestamp`. Leave
49	body content intact unless a file mixes types — then split it.
50	
51	**Gate (incremental):** after converting each batch,
52	`mix okf.manifest <root> && mix okf.validate <root>` and clear every `FAIL:`. The validator
53	catches the conversion's typical misses: missing `type`, vague/short `description`,
54	leftover `<placeholder>`, bad `timestamp`, broken cross-link.
55	
56	## 3. Preserve provenance
57	- A file that was an immutable capture → `type: source`; **stop editing its body**.
58	- Deep history stays in git: the conversion is a commit, so `git log` preserves the past.
59	- Convert ad-hoc cross-references into markdown links to the new paths. (The validator
60	  fails on any link whose target file doesn't exist — use it to find the ones you missed.)
61	
62	**Gate:** `mix okf.validate <root>` reports **zero** `broken link` failures.
63	
64	## 4. Decide the tier per document (default: floor)
65	Everything lands on the **OKF floor** unless [`standard/tiers.md`](standard/tiers.md)'s boundary
66	test trips; only then add `tier: cb`, `id`, `deps`. If the source was already a CB/belief
67	graph (e.g. `assertions.json`), **keep it as the canonical store and emit an OKF
68	projection** (each belief → a typed `concept` doc; `deps` → cross-links + `deps`
69	frontmatter). Don't flatten a belief graph into prose and lose the edges.
70	
71	**Gate:** for every `tier: cb` doc, `mix okf.validate` confirms it has an `id` and ids are
72	unique (it FAILs otherwise). Cross-bundle `deps` may show as `WARN` — that's expected.
73	
74	## 5. Final manifest + acceptance test
75	```
76	mix okf.manifest <root> && mix okf.validate <root>
77	```
78	Must exit 0. Then pick **three** task-shaped questions a future agent might ask, and
79	confirm each is answerable from `manifest.json` + at most two docs. If not, the
80	`description`s are too vague — fix them, not the bodies, and re-run.
81	
82	## 6. Report
83	Summarize: counts by `type`, what stayed floor vs went CB, anything you could not place
84	(flag for the human), the new entry `index.md`, and confirmation that `mix okf.validate` exits
85	0. Note that the original remains in git history for any unconverted detail.

1	# ADOPT — bootstrapping a repo onto the Knowledge methodology
2	
3	You are an agent told: *"adopt this methodology for this repo."* Work through the steps.
4	**Each mutating step ends in a gate you run — do not proceed past a failing gate.** The
5	tooling lives in the `composable-beliefs` repo (the OKF integration layer beside this
6	`okf/` directory); run these from the repo root against the target bundle path:
7	
8	```
9	mix okf.manifest <root>   # (re)generate <root>/manifest.json
10	mix okf.validate <root>   # exit 0 = pass; prints FAIL: lines + a count
11	```
12	
13	`mix okf.validate` is the source of truth for "did that step actually work." Treat
14	a non-zero exit, or any `FAIL:` line, as a stop condition. `WARN:` lines are advisory.
15	
16	## 0. Read the spec
17	Read in order: [`standard/KNOWLEDGE.md`](standard/KNOWLEDGE.md), [`standard/types.md`](standard/types.md),
18	[`standard/frontmatter.md`](standard/frontmatter.md), [`standard/tiers.md`](standard/tiers.md). Skim
19	[`demo/`](demo/) for a worked example and [`meta/`](meta/) for this repo's design record.
20	
21	**Gate (self-check):** before continuing, you must be able to answer without re-reading:
22	(a) which field is the relevance hook, (b) the boundary test that promotes a doc to the
23	CB tier, (c) what `manifest.json` is for. If you can't, re-read.
24	
25	## 1. Decide the bundle root
26	Pick where knowledge lives in the target repo — typically `knowledge/` or `docs/`. This
27	is the **bundle root** (`<root>` below). No tooling is copied into the target repo: the
28	`mix okf.*` tasks in the `composable-beliefs` repo operate on any bundle path.
29	
30	**Gate:** from the `composable-beliefs` repo root, `mix help okf.validate` prints usage
31	(confirms the tasks are available).
32	
33	## 2. Create the skeleton
34	```
35	<root>/
36	├── index.md            # type: index — what this repo's knowledge covers
37	├── <domain>/           # one folder per natural domain
38	│   └── index.md
39	└── meta/               # threads, plans, analyses, positions about the repo itself
40	    └── index.md
41	```
42	Copy [`templates/`](templates/) as starting points. **Fill every `<placeholder>`** —
43	unfilled `<...>` in frontmatter is a hard failure. Default every doc to the **OKF floor**
44	(omit `tier`); reach for `tier: cb` only when [`standard/tiers.md`](standard/tiers.md)'s boundary
45	test trips (and then the doc needs an `id`).
46	
47	**Gate:**
48	```
49	mix okf.manifest <root> && mix okf.validate <root>
50	```
51	Must exit 0. Fix every `FAIL:` (missing `type`, weak `description`, leftover placeholder,
52	broken link) before continuing.
53	
54	## 3. Author the entry index
55	`<root>/index.md`'s `description` is the relevance hook for the **whole repo's
56	knowledge** — one line that tells a fresh agent whether this repo is relevant to its task.
57	Link from it to each domain's `index.md`.
58	
59	**Gate:** re-run step 2's command (exit 0). Then confirm `manifest.json`'s first entry
60	for `index.md` has a `description` you'd be willing to route on.
61	
62	## 4. Backfill initial content
63	Populate domains with real `concept`/`reference`/`source` docs (not just indexes). Every
64	new or changed file means a regenerate + validate.
65	
66	**Gate (the loop):** after any edit, run
67	`mix okf.manifest <root> && mix okf.validate <root>` until it exits 0 with zero `FAIL:`.
68	
69	## 5. The standing workflow (day-to-day, after adoption)
70	- **End of a working session** → write one `type: thread` doc in `meta/`; its
71	  `description` is the outcome, linking to what it produced. (Stops ad-hoc threads.)
72	- **New external input** → capture as `type: source` (immutable), then **update the
73	  relevant `type: concept` docs** — don't let sources pile up unsynthesized.
74	- **A settled stance** → `type: position`; consider CB-tier promotion.
75	- **After any change** → `mix okf.manifest <root> && mix okf.validate <root>`, exit 0.
76	- **Wire it so it can't drift:** add `mix okf.validate <root>` to CI or a pre-commit
77	  hook. (`mix okf.manifest <root> --check` alone catches only manifest staleness;
78	  `mix okf.validate` catches that *and* schema/link breakage.)
79	
80	## 6. Done when (acceptance test)
81	Adoption is complete only when **all** hold:
82	1. `mix okf.validate <root>` exits 0 (no `FAIL:`).
83	2. `<root>/manifest.json` exists and lists every doc with a real `description`.
84	3. Pick **three** task-shaped questions a future agent might ask this repo. Each must be

content.content:
=== does cb.import support supersedes? ===

=== cb.adjudicate usage ===

                               mix cb.adjudicate

Apply a captured belief-conflict adjudication to the DAG.

Reads a single adjudication record (the wire format the preflight step writes
after the user chooses `accept_supersede | reject_dep_tie | defer`) and invokes
`CB.Belief.Adjudication.apply/2` to perform the atomic write.

The wire format is:

    {
      "proposed": { ...belief fields, no id... },
      "conflicting_id": "c029",
      "outcome": "accept_supersede" | "reject_dep_tie" | "defer",
      "reasoning": "user-provided text",
      "session_ref": "review-session-2026-04-21"
    }

## Usage

    mix cb.adjudicate --file /tmp/cb-adjudication-....json

## Exit codes

  * `0` success
  * `1` invalid input (missing fields, bad outcome, malformed JSON, missing
    file, conflicting id not found in DAG)
  * `2` internal conflict - the conflicting belief's status moved off
    `active` between adjudication capture and this task running, a race we
    refuse to compound

content.text:
The rename is complete, verified, and pushed (`0720a29`).

## Rename report: `mix knowledge.* → mix okf.*`

**Code** — `mix okf.{emit,ingest,manifest,validate}` (old tasks gone), modules `CB.Knowledge.* → CB.Okf.*` (`lib/cb/knowledge/ → lib/cb/okf/`), tests renamed (`okf_test.exs`, conformance alias updated). Methodology nouns ("Knowledge"/"OKF") left intact — only command/module identifiers changed.

**Hand-written docs** — `ADOPT`/`CONVERT`/`README`/`conformance/README`/`KNOWLEDGE.md`/`manifest.json` updated to `mix okf.*` and `lib/cb/okf/*`.

**Graph (the necessary tail)** — three render-section beliefs named the old commands and *compile verbatim into `okf/CLAUDE.md`*, so leaving them would have served dead commands. Superseded through the write flow (`cb.adjudicate accept_supersede`):
- `okfx:a003 → a011` — commands renamed **and** its stale `knowledge/beliefs/ → okf/beliefs.json` path fixed, which **discharges `okfx:a010`**
- `okfx:a007 → a012`, `okfx:a008 → a013`
- contract `okfx:c001 → c002` — render_sections + deps repointed at the successors

`okf/CLAUDE.md` regenerated (0 `mix knowledge.*`, 3 `mix okf.*`). `okfx:a010` materialized + closed.

**Left deliberately** (recorded in a010's discharge notes): `okfx:a004` (a discharged directive — its `knowledge/CLAUDE.md` is historical) and `okfx:a005` (an illustrative "e.g." command name). Both non-compiled; superseding them would be disproportionate churn against immutability.

**Verified green** (independently): `mix test` 362/0, both `cb.generate.claude_md --check` paths, `cb.verify.collection okfx`, `cb.verify.schema`, `mix okf.validate okf/demo`. Clean sweep — no `mix knowledge.*`/`CB.Knowledge`/`lib/cb/knowledge` in live code or docs.

**okfx desk now:** `okfx:a001`, `okfx:a002`, `okfx:a005` (the CONVERT pilot, the threads backfill, and the conformance-regen task). Nothing needs you.

One note: this session did decision-weight graph work (the four supersessions + two discharges, grounded in your rename directive), so per the persistence discipline it should get its transcript + chronicle recorded. Want me to `/end` to persist that, or are you continuing with more follow-ups first?

content.text: Yes — ready. Working tree is clean, everything's committed and pushed; the remaining `/end` work is persisting this session's record (transcript, chronicle, retro-pairs) per the decision-weight discipline. Running it:

content.content: Launching skill: end

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/mark/.claude/skills/end

# /end - close the session with nothing left only in the chat

Invoking this skill is the user's authorization to commit and push the
persistence artifacts it produces (per repo git policy, which otherwise
requires explicit instruction). Destructive operations still confirm.

Pull all touched repos before writing anything: other sessions write these
repos concurrently, and obligations are authored against the live graph,
never from memory (cb:a504).

## Step 1 - worktree and tree hygiene

If the session ran in a worktree, merge to main and clean up (orphans are
/merge-worktrees' job). Otherwise confirm working trees are clean or account
for every uncommitted file before proceeding.

## Step 2 - thread sweep: what exists only in this conversation?

Re-read the thread for: decisions made, conventions settled, findings and
incidents, stances argued, direction content worked through, obligations
created or discovered, tool facts learned, and agent reasoning errors the
user caught. For each, check whether it is already persisted (graph node,
plan record, position doc, operations note, direction doc). Route the
unpersisted by proportionality (cb:a496):

- **Direction content - strategy, positioning, competitor evaluation,
  audience/market analysis, monetization and distribution thinking** ->
  NEVER a public-facing surface: not a README, not docs/, not positions/,
  not chronicles/, not a public graph, not a blog draft. It routes to
  exactly one of two private repos, by subject, as a dated doc in that
  repo's `direction/`:
  - CB-the-system (its positioning, competitors, distribution) ->
    `composablebeliefs/cb-direction`
  - amieval and everything else (the broader operation, cross-system
    strategy) -> `amieval/amieval-direction`
  These two are the only categorical direction destinations; do not create
  others. When a piece mixes checkable claims with strategy, split it: the
  checkable part graduates to the public surface, the strategic part goes
  to direction. Rationale on record in cb-direction's README and
  `direction/2026-06-12-claim-the-eval-vertical.md`: publishing the
  strategic rationale undercuts the credibility the public positioning
  pursues.
- **Stance whose reasoning matters verbatim** -> position document (in the
  repo whose system it is about, per cb:a492), then extract claims into the
  dag through the write flow.
- **Finding or convention with an obvious prescription** -> directive
  straight into the dag (gap as rationale prose or a small design-gap node).
- **Obligation / future work** -> backlog directive: kind action-item,
  tag `lifecycle:discrete`, grounded in deps or a stipulation artifact.
  Verify it is not already done or already on the desk before minting.
- **Tool facts** (editor behaviors, CLI quirks) -> evidence detail on the
  relevant convention node and/or docs/operations.md. Never claims.
- **Agent reasoning errors caught by the user** -> error-pattern node or
  evidence append. Look for rejected approaches ("no, that's wrong
  because..."), missed existing patterns ("we already have X"), unverified
  assumptions, and implicit corrections (the user restating a requirement
  after a misread). Link to existing patterns first (`mix bs list
  kind:error-pattern`, `kind:reasoning-error`); a recurring shape with no
  match seeds a new node, subject `{"ref": "agent", "type": "agent"}`.
  Distinguish what happened from why: observed behavior is a primitive
  ("observed: X"); a theory about the agent's reasoning is a compound
  ("theory: X"). Evidence detail tells the specific story ("agent proposed
  concatenated location field"), never the generic one ("agent made a
  modeling error"). Don't over-assert: one-off slips that feed no future
  reasoning stay in the chat.

All graph writes go through the front door: preflight, record the
tag-overlap review in the evidence detail (overlap is necessary but never
sufficient for conflict), import, verify. Never hand-edit a graph file in a
repo that has a write flow.

## Step 3 - dag routing

Home each belief by the boundary: claims about CB-the-system land in `cb:`
(composable-beliefs); mission, domain, and field-use content lands in the
appropriate belief-collections collection (operator field observations carry
the `anecdote` tag per paradigm:a368; meta-observations about the human-agent
system go to `paradigm:`). Discover what dags exist via `collections.json`
and sibling `manifest.json` files rather than assuming.

- If content belongs to several dags and the homes are clear, write each
  piece to its home and say so.
- If the homing is ambiguous, ask the user, with a recommendation.
- If a touched repo carries **no dag**, tell the user explicitly. Distinguish
  "missing" from "no dag by design" (cb-site carries none deliberately:
  proto-content churns; settled claims graduate to positions later).

## Step 4 - session memory audit

Session memory is an ephemeral cache (cb:a509): project and work state is
banned from it. Scan the memory directory for anything load-bearing that
lives only there and promote it through the write flow (the cb:a504-a506
precedent). Then PRUNE: delete the project-state files, leaving MEMORY.md as
a one-line pointer at the graph (CLAUDE.md Session start + the desk). At
most a thin operator-preferences note survives, and only until the private
overlay collection gives preferences a graph home.

## Step 5 - transcript, chronicle, and scratch graduation

If the session did plan-scale work, persist or postscript its transcript in
the co-located plans convention (plans/<set>/transcript-execution.md).

If the session was **decision-weight** (cb:a540) - it minted or superseded
beliefs grounded in a user:/session: stipulation, settled a stance, or
adjudicated contradictory positions - also persist the thread **verbatim**.
cb:a518 is now landed, so this is a **file duplication, never a
reconstruction**: copy the real session log - the uuid-keyed `.jsonl` for this
session under `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project>/` (the file whose name
matches this session's id, newest by mtime) - byte-for-byte into the
subject-appropriate repo as an immutable `type: source` doc, following the
knowledge standard's source+thread persistence rule
(amieval/knowledge `standard/types.md`). Home it by subject: strategy/direction
threads to the private direction repos (amieval-direction / cb-direction, by
the cb-direction routing rule), plan-scale dev threads to plans/<set>/. A
continuation of an existing arc appends to that arc's thread dir rather than
starting a new one. The copied `.jsonl` is what every `session:` artifact now
resolves to, so retro-pair each belief minted this session (cb:a507) with a
`document:` pointer to it. The directive stays the SSOT and self-bootstrapping;
the verbatim thread is provenance only.

Then write or extend the thread's **chronicle** (cb:a520): a dated prose
narrative in chronicles/ of the repo where the thread centered - where
things stood, the arc with its incidents as story beats, where things stand
now, what the next session inherits. Written for the operator's steering,
never as a dry event list: narrative carries the load, ids stay subordinate
(the inverse of the receipts register). The transcript is the record; the
chronicle is how the human keeps up.
Graduate the tmp/ glue surfaces (lap-log, transcript.md, cards): anything
they surfaced that matters moves to the transcript or the graph; the scratch
files themselves stay untracked and die. Adjudication wire records and
proposal files graduate to the proposals/ dir of the thread's
center-of-gravity plan set - the graph carries the adjudication, the file
carries the provenance.

After the chronicle and transcript exist, retro-pair: append a `document:`
pointer to any evidence minted this session that cites a bare `session:`
slug (cb:a507 - a session ref is a dead end for a fresh agent until the
persistence pipeline lands; cb:a518 is the systemic fix).

## Step 6 - blog packaging (cb-site)

Package learnings that apply to CB or amieval into dated drafts in
`cb-site/posts/drafts/` (private repo: composablebeliefs/cb-site). Instruction,
verbatim from the operator:

> these are as much for me to read to understand what the functionality now
> is as to explain how we got here and why decisions were made as they were.
> this should be less of a sequential recounting and more of an actual blog
> post. concepts should be explained, assume an audience that does not yet
> know what cb is, though is technical enough to read posts with code. do
> not worry about writerly flourishes, do not try to "write", instead focus
> on relaying what you think is consequential in a straightforward style

Separate posts by topic, never by chronology. One draft per genuinely
distinct learning; skip sessions that produced none (routine work is not a
post). Mark each as Draft with origin and audience at the top. House prose
rules apply: no emdashes; state what things are directly. Report performance
claims as anecdote until an eval says otherwise.

Drafts are publication-bound: no direction content (strategy, positioning,
competitor evaluation) in a draft - that routes to the direction repos per
step 2, even when it would make good narrative.

## Step 7 - verify, commit, report

For every touched repo, run its gates before committing: `mix test`,
`mix cb.verify.schema`, `mix cb.generate.claude_md --check`,
`mix cb.verify.collection <ns>` for touched collections. Leave green or
report exactly what is red and why.

Commit per repo with messages that say what landed and why; push. Close with
the session report:

- what was persisted where (ids, files, commits)
- the live desk: `mix bs list unlinked tag:lifecycle:discrete` per relevant
  graph (note the cross-collection gap, cb:a500, until it is built)
- anything that needs the user: ambiguous homings, red gates, decisions
  deferred

## Step 8 - the close sweeps itself

The close itself surfaces findings: calibration issues, machinery friction,
skill stumbles, observations "worth carrying forward." Persist them before
reporting them - a finding that lives only in the final chat message is the
exact failure this skill exists to prevent. Route by step 2's rules (usually
a desk directive or an evidence append on the node it concerns), then let
the report reference the id.
I'll work through the /end steps. **Step 1 — pull touched repos and confirm tree hygiene:**
